food_subgroup: Cooked crustaceans — wok-seared prawns with aromatics and seasonings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for wok-seared or dry-pot prawns; closest parent 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC 'Crustaceans, cooked' is the closest defensible parent; no specific code exists for wok-seared dry-pot prawn preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888009 'Prawn (food)' is the closest available concept; no specific code for wok-seared preparation exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of wok-seared prawns is approximately 250 g, comprising 4–6 whole prawns with shells and heads, cooked with aromatics and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Prawns, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150) adapted with cooking-oil and seasoning additions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central 'Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat' (FDC ID 175180). Values adjusted upward for fat and sodium to account for wok-searing in cooking oil (~10 g oil per 100 g edible portion) and seasoning (soy sauce, oyster sauce). Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. This is a standard adaptation methodology consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo approach for cooked seafood dishes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; shellfish and seafood reference values
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for wok-seared dry-pot prawns. Prawns and shrimp are protein-dominant, very low-carbohydrate foods. The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, Am J Clin Nutr) consistently classify shellfish and crustaceans with GI ≤ 5 due to negligible glycemic carbohydrate content. GI of 5 assigned as analogous estimate for cooked prawn dishes with minimal added carbohydrate. GL per 250 g serving = (5 × 3.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 0.44, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~520 mg per 100 g; ~1300 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving and request reduced-sauce preparation, as a full 250 g serving contributes approximately 1300 mg sodium — over half the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI ~5) and low carbohydrate content (~3.5 g per 100 g) with high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Wok-seared prawns are suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to their very low GI, minimal carbohydrate load, and high protein content, which supports glycaemic control; however, the sodium content from seasonings warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated dietary cholesterol (~152 mg per 100 g) and moderate sodium; partially offset by favourable omega-3 fatty acid profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume wok-seared prawns in moderation — no more than once or twice per week — given the relatively high cholesterol content (~380 mg per serving), while noting that the polyunsaturated fat profile and lean protein are cardioprotective when sodium intake is managed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wok-Seared Prawns (干锅虾, Gān Guō Xiā) is a popular Singaporean Chinese seafood dish in which whole fresh prawns — typically with shells and heads intact — are cooked at high heat in a seasoned wok with aromatics including garlic, ginger, dried chillies, and spring onions, then finished with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. The dish is characterised by a smoky, slightly charred exterior from the intense wok heat (known as 'wok hei'), a juicy and tender prawn interior, and a savoury-umami sauce that clings to the shells. The preparation is classified as minimally processed, using whole crustaceans with added seasonings and cooking oil. Nutritionally, wok-seared prawns are a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate food with approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, 17.8 g protein, and 7.2 g fat. The dish has a very low glycemic index (GI ~5) making it suitable for glycaemic management. Sodium content is elevated (~520 mg per 100 g) due to soy-based sauces, which is a key consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is a good source of selenium, phosphorus, vitamin B12, and iron. It is served as a main course in Singaporean hawker centres, zi char restaurants, and casual dining establishments, typically priced at SGD 14–18 per plate. Common allergens include crustaceans, shellfish, soy, and sesame.
common_user_queries: How many calories in dry pot prawns?
Is gan guo xia high in sodium?

Nutrition facts for wok fried prawns Singapore

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked shrimp data (FDC ID 175180) with adjustments for wok-cooking oil and soy-based seasonings. No exact HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this specific dish was identified. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed shellfish GI literature. Confidence is medium rather than high because the nutrient profile is adapted rather than directly measured for this specific restaurant preparation.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source for cooked shrimp and is not reliably estimated for this preparation. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked shrimp with vegetable cooking oil — no industrial trans fats are present. The dish name '干锅虾' (Gān Guō Xiā) literally means 'dry pot prawns' and refers to the dry-wok cooking technique rather than a soup or broth preparation. The restaurant source (Hock Kee Braised Beef Noodles, Lorong Telok) is a Singapore hawker-style establishment; this dish appears as a supplementary seafood offering. Price SGD 16.28 preserved from input record. Nutrient values represent an edible-portion estimate inclusive of sauce absorption; shell and head weight are excluded from the per-100g calculation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are carried from USDA FDC 175180 with proportional adjustment for added oil.
